segs.utf8:
Transcriber: NGUYỄN HƯỚNG
Reviewer: Vaia Katsarou

A few years ago,
when my husband was my boyfriend,
he used to travel a lot for work,
and when he did, I would have a ritual.
I would binge watch Law and Order.
I'm just going to pause for a moment

so you can all hear the “dun dun”
in your head.

There’s something oddly soothing
about that sound.

For a show that’s all about
crime and violence,

I think it’s soothing because
it has such a predictable formula.

For those of you who aren't
familiar with the formula,
it goes something like this:
something really horrible happens,

a murder, an abduction,
something really bad.
But then help arrives.
The detectives show up.
They gather the clues,
they ask the questions,

and they do this all
with a singular purpose:

to take the horrible thing that happened
and make it right again.

Now, the fanfare of the show
is mostly focused on the detectives,

but I've always had a soft spot
for the second half of the formula.
For the lawyers.

Once the detectives are done
gathering all the information,

it’s the lawyers
that take that information,

and use the law and the rules
to fight for what’s right.

For me in the show, it was always
kind of the law that was the hero.

Now, I loved living in that world
for 42 minutes at a time,

but in the real world we know
that law is not always the hero.
Sometimes law is the villain.

It's been a couple of years
since my binge watching days.

I’m now a professor of Political Science
and International Affairs
at Penn State.

I hold an appointment
with the Peace Research Institute in Oslo,
and I co-host The Democracy Works podcast
for the McCourtney Institute for Democracy
I research and I teach about Human Rights,

and the painful,  often violent, moments
in which those rights are violated.
I still watch Law and Order,

and I still give a little cheer
when the lawyers enter the room,

but today, I want to talk to you
about the cases
in which law is not the hero.

I want to talk to you
about something called “lawfare”.
When instead of protecting our rights,

laws punish us and take
those rights away instead.

Most of us grow up thinking
about laws as neutral or impartial.

We believe that laws are created
to keep us safe and to keep us free.
But here’s the truth: laws are just rules,

and rules are only as fair or impartial
as the people who put them in place.

If any of you have ever watched a group
of seven year olds on the playground?
You know exactly what I'm talking about.

There's always one kid that wants
to make up a rule for everybody else.

I’m in charge, and you can only play
if I say you can play,

or I’m the queen and we’re all going to do
exactly what I want.
Or today we’re all going to be hamsters.

These rules are random,
uneven, even ridiculous,
but they all have a very clear purpose:

they benefit the person
who’s putting the rules in place.
And adults aren't much better.
Our laws are just rules

that are put in place to benefit
the person that’s creating them.

They can be fair and impartial,
designed to protect,

or uneven and arbitrary,
designed to control.

And lawfare is when the rule maker
puts rules in place that benefit them.
It’s when the government designs laws

that are meant to take our rights away
instead of protecting our rights.

Now, when we think about governments
taking our rights away,

we tend to think about things like
torture, assassinations, slavery.
Lawfare doesn’t look like that.

Lawfare doesn't look
like tanks in the streets,

or a dictator pounding
his fist on a balcony.

Lawfare looks like court decisions
like fines, like administrative burdens.
Lawfare looks deceptively normal,
and that is what makes it so dangerous.

Lawfare is happening
all around the world today.
In democracies and autocracies,

in wealthy nations,
and in developing ones.
No country is immune.

In Bangladesh, new rules
have been put in place

that make it almost impossible
for civil society organizations

that advocate for press freedoms
to exist legally.
In Hungary, new laws have been passed

that prevent the gathering of groups
that advocate for LGBTQ rights.

And in China, simply posting a meme
that’s derogatory towards the government

can have you punished
under expansive cybersecurity laws.

In such cases, the laws are not protecting
the citizens of those countries.
The laws are protecting the government.

Now, lawfare is relatively new,
but the idea behind it is not at all.

When we think about governments
committing human rights violations,

we tend to think about
dramatic, violent events.

We think about things like
the Holocaust or the Russian gulags
or the Rwandan Genocide.
Governments have always taken away rights,

and in each of these events,
millions of people lost their lives.
But they also lost their rights,

their rights to protest,
to assemble, to vote,
to engage independent media,
and to seek protection from the police.
And these were not events

that were committed
by fringe extremists, or criminal groups,

these were events
that were committed by governments,
by institutions,

by individuals wearing uniforms
and following the rules.

Conservative estimates suggest
that governments

killed over 200 million
of their own citizens
in the 20th century.

That is more people
than the total deaths of World War I
and World War II combined.
But there is some good news.

Following signing of the
UN Declaration of Human Rights in 1948,

overt physical violence by governments
has been on the decline.
But this decline isn't the whole story.

Because governments
didn't stop abusing rights,

they just got quieter
about how they do it.
Let me tell you how that happened.

Following World War ΙΙ
the world changed dramatically.

We created new institutions
like the United Nations,

we signed new treaties
like the Convention Against Torture,
and governments around the world
promised that never again

would they stand by
and watch other governments

abuse their citizens
at such atrocious levels.
For the first time in human history,

governments were paying attention
to other governments.

Civil society groups
had broad global networks,

journalists could share stories
around the world almost instantly,
and citizens had new tools

that allowed them to record abuses
and to share them.

We were better informed
and better protected than ever before,

and people use that information to vote,
to boycott, to divest,

and to shame governments
into better behavior.

For a moment, it really looked
like human rights were going to triumph
over government power.
But then governments adapted.
Governments decided that it was too costly
to use conventional strategies of violence

to violate human rights
and maintain their power.
And so instead of breaking the law,
governments started to change the law.
Instead of using soldiers and guns,

governments started using
courtrooms and paperwork.
Why send soldiers in to break up a protest

when you can simply change the law
to make that protest illegal?

Why jail journalists
when you can create new rules
that ban them from the press room?
And why shut down universities

when you can change accreditation rules
to control what’s being taught?
No bloodshed, no condemnation,
and technically no broken laws.

I want to tell you the story
of Alexei Navalny.

Navalny was a prominent
opposition politician in Russia.

He was a tireless advocate
for democracy and human rights.

Navalny exposed the corruption
and injustices of Putin's regime.

Navalny directly
threatened the Putin government
and challenged his hold on power.

Now, the Russian government
could have had Navalny assassinated,

but that ran the risk of drawing
international condemnation

and making Navalny a martyr
for the cause of democracy in Russia.

So instead, the Russian
government used lawfare.

Navalny was charged
with fraud and embezzlement

through Russia’s expansive
cybersecurity laws.
And through these laws,

while we could all see that they were
incredibly politically motivated,

it made it really difficult
for civil society

and foreign governments
to rally to Navalny’s aid,

because, after all,  Russia
was allowed to uphold its own laws.

The Russian government
used lawfare against Alexei Navalny,
and Navalny died in prison two years ago.

Lawfare is a quieter form of repression,
but it is no less brutal,
and that is what makes it so effective.

Lawfare takes laws and uses
many of the same careful legal forms

and legal language as the laws
that are designed to protect our rights.
Lawfare looks legitimate on paper.

Now, as a social scientist,
I really struggle

because lawfare is so incredibly
difficult to measure.

We can observe human rights abuses
in newspaper articles or NGO reports,

but it's very difficult
to get at a government's intent.
Let me give you some examples.

Anti-terror laws are
an incredibly effective strategy
at protecting national security interests,

but they can also be used
to criminalize dissent.

Election reform can be useful
for preventing voter fraud,

but it can also be used to systematically
disenfranchised targeted populations.
And categorizing or cataloging groups
can be a really important mechanism

for distributing social services
and other types of benefits,

but it’s also the first step
towards a genocide.
Lawfare is incredibly difficult to measure

but I try to remember
that hiding their behaviors

is exactly what governments
are trying to do.
This is a scary moment for the law.

We can no longer pretend
that when something horrible happens,

lawyers are going to swoop in
like those from my favorite TV show.

But the 20th century has given us
something incredibly important.

The history of human rights has given us
a strategy that we know works.
Following World War II,

governments didn’t change their behavior
because they wanted to,

they changed their behavior
because people were paying attention.
Global attention changed behavior.
And so we have a strategy right now

for resisting lawfare in our own country
and around the world.
The first step to resisting lawfare
is to be able to recognize lawfare.
Lawfare draws its power

because we all assume
that laws are neutral.

But remember those seven
year olds on the playground.

If you know that laws can be weaponized
to support the person in power,

then you are already way ahead
in your ability to identify

and challenge these rules
when you see them.

The second step to resisting lawfare
is to pay attention to the judiciary.
The judiciary is the branch of government
that interprets and upholds the law.

In many parts of the world,
like the United States,
judicial positions are elected.

We can vote for local judges,
even state Supreme Court justices.

These are the people that shape
the laws that shape our lives.

And while it may not be
particularly glamorous

to research which judge you’re going
to vote for in the next election cycle,
judges matter.

Courts matter, and your vote
should reflect your preferences.

The third step to resisting lawfare is
to become an engaged global citizen.

Dr Martin Luther King Jr said
that injustice anywhere
is a threat to justice everywhere.

This is not just a moral statement,
but a political truth.

Raise your awareness,
engage global events,

and that will teach you
the lessons that you need

to identify and resist
lawfare in your own backyard.
I still watch Law and Order,

and I still get a little excited
when the lawyers rush in
to that bright, wood paneled courtroom,

but real life is not
a predictable TV drama.

The law is powerful,
but it's very fragile.

And the law becomes dangerous
when we stop paying attention to it.
Our job is to keep the law honest,
to keep the law accountable,

to ensure that the law will be a tool
for justice and not injustice,
and to ensure that the law
helps and supports our human rights,
not take them away.

The law can still be
the hero of our story,

but only if we insist
on being the hero behind it.
Thank you.
(Applause)
